title: Testing, Static Quality, Performance, and Release · Runtime Trace: Release Evidence
summary: Harden the service-data client with behavioral tests, deterministic fixtures, performance evidence, and a clean release. Planned artifact: Add and verify the js release evidence increment in a tested and release-ready JavaScript application. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Produce a clean-environment build, supported-runtime record, accessibility checks, rollback plan, and stakeholder acceptance evidence.
Measure startup, long tasks, memory retention, network, rendering, and algorithm cost before optimizing a bottleneck.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

// javascript-basics cumulative stakeholder program.

steps.title: Predict Release Evidence
steps.instruction:
theory case — A release candidate passes the familiar 3-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Release Evidence contract for the supplied data.

content.text: Produce a clean-environment build, supported-runtime record, accessibility checks, rollback plan, and stakeholder acceptance evidence. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Trace concrete runtime values, bindings, receiver, mutation, and queued work in order; assert the observable result, then change one input or event and seek the first causal divergence.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: A release candidate passes the familiar 3-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees. A defensible solution applies Release Evidence, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a tested and release-ready JavaScript application.

content.text: A successful local run is sufficient release proof. This fails the Release Evidence contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.

steps.title: Defend the Release Evidence model
steps.instruction: Write the theory evidence note for Release Evidence: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A release candidate passes the familiar 4-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Produce a clean-environment build, supported-runtime record, accessibility checks, rollback plan, and stakeholder acceptance evidence.
